description: A mechanic is a feature that the game provides such that the player is able to accomplish game given tasks. This is not the same as what you do.
name: mechanics

children.description:
the game includes AI companions that accompany the player (if there is a player character) for some time

NOTICE: does not include escort missions
children.name: ai companions
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / ai companions

children.description: the game has AI companions that can be commanded by the player (fully or partially)
children.name: ai: commandable
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / ai companions / ai: commandable

children.description:
the game has AI companions that CANNOT be commanded by the player

includes all types of companions as long as it is not only an escort mission and the companion follows the player
children.name: ai: uncommandable
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / ai companions / ai: uncommandable

children.description: how communication is handled in the game
children.name: communication
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication

children.description:
Communication is bound to a location, e.g. players can only hear each other when they are close to each other in the game world.
This is allowed to be tagged for single player games.
children.name: com: proximity communication
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / com: proximity communication

children.description: which type of communication with NPCs is available
children.name: npc communication
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / npc communication

children.description: NPCs talk to each other (e.g. ambient dialog or cutscenes) which can act as a source of information for the player
children.name: com: npc and npc
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / npc communication / com: npc and npc

children.description: NPCs communicate with the player (not their in-game character), e.g. in a tutorial. This can include narrators.
children.name: com: npc and player
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / npc communication / com: npc and player

children.description: The players can communicate with NPCs via their in-game character, e.g. to gather information, complete quests, trade resources or just chat
children.name: com: npc and playercharacter
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / npc communication / com: npc and playercharacter

children.description: which types of player-to-player communication is available
children.name: player communication
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication

children.description: communcation with some degree of free expression
children.name: free communication
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / free communication

children.description: players communicate outside of the game, e.g. because they are in the same location or by using other software
children.name: com: external
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / free communication / com: external

children.description: players can draw to communicate with other players, e.g. by drawing on a map to indicate where something is
children.name: com: free drawing
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / free communication / com: free drawing

children.description: players can communicate via free gestures (most likely only VR games)
children.name: com: free gestures
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / free communication / com: free gestures

children.description: players can communicate through a text chat
children.name: com: text chat
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / free communication / com: text chat

children.description: players can communicate through a voice chat
children.name: com: voice chat
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / free communication / com: voice chat

children.description: communcation over presets, i.e., no free expression
children.name: preset communication
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / preset communication

children.description:
players can post premade messages to a chat, usually by using a wheel where they can choose between a set of available messages

This acts as a barrier for toxicity and does not require devs to implement chat filters.
children.name: com: chat wheel
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / preset communication / com: chat wheel

children.description: players can communicate using a set of predefined gestures their character can make
children.name: com: gestures
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / preset communication / com: gestures

children.description: players can communicate through markers and pings, i.e., small symbolic representations
children.name: com: markers and pings
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / preset communication / com: markers and pings

children.description: players can communicate through a sound board, e.g., through a set of predefined spoken lines (voiced by someone else)
children.name: com: soundboard
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / communication / player communication / preset communication / com: soundboard

children.description: how/when the game is over
children.name: game over condition
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / game over condition

children.description: the game is over when the player fails critically, e.g. death, which resets to a previous point in the game, thereby losing any progress between this point and the critical failure
children.name: goc: critical failure
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / game over condition / goc: critical failure

children.description: there is no direct game over condition, e.g., because the game continues after the story ends (Minecraft) or because there was no end to begin with (endless mode)
children.name: goc: none
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / game over condition / goc: none

children.description: When the player dies, the game resets to the start, as is common for rogue-like games. This does not include games where the player can simply load, that would just be a critical failure.
children.name: goc: permadeath
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / game over condition / goc: permadeath

children.description: The game ends when a certain point is reached, e.g., the conclusion of the main quest. A game ending means that the game can not be played further without starting anew.
children.name: goc: reach endpoint
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / game over condition / goc: reach endpoint

children.description: The game has mechanics based around card games. This can be actual card games or just prominent elements of card games.
children.name: mech: card game
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: card game

children.description: the game has (optional) content intended to challenge players' skill, e.g. challenge dungeons
children.name: mech: challenges
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: challenges

children.description: players can change the speed of the game, e.g., through slow motion/bullet time, by having a tactical pause or directly controling the speed (slower/faster)
children.name: mech: change game speed
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: change game speed

children.description: the game has combos in some sense, e.g., when players successfully chain or combine (specific) attacks, when players can craft combos, etc.
children.name: mech: combos
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: combos

children.description: The player can configure some type of quick access. This could be a hotbar or a radial menu where items/skills can be assigned.
children.name: mech: configurable quick access
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: configurable quick access

children.description: for any missing tags related to mechanics
children.name: mech: other
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: other

children.description:
there are physical objects in the game where the player can save and/or rest, optionally with replenishing their health

saving can mean creating a new save file or getting a new checkpoint
children.name: mech: physical save points
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: physical save points

children.description: players can place and arrange objects inside the game world, usually employed for building or moving units
children.name: mech: place & arrange
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: place & arrange

children.description: the game provides information in the form of notes, audio diaries, encylopedias, letters, etc.
children.name: mech: predefined notes
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: predefined notes

children.description: players can see their current quest objectives/tasks displayed on the screen (automatically or by setting a quest as active)
children.name: mech: quest tracker display
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: quest tracker display

children.description: players can queue specific actions like movement or more complex commands; does not count if the game only internally uses a queue, it needs to be visually present or possible to see for the player
children.name: mech: queue
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: queue

children.description: the game contains events that require players to press a specific button in a given time window; can have bad consequences if failed
children.name: mech: quicktime events
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: quicktime events

children.description: the game includes systems, like a skill or research tree, where upgrades are successively unlocked by the player
children.name: mech: skilltree & upgrades
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: skilltree & upgrades

children.description: the game has stealth mechanics like sneaking, wearing disguises or stealing
children.name: mech: stealth
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: stealth

children.description: players can transform things inside the game into other things, e.g. crafting (this does not including paying for things with money)
children.name: mech: transform
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: transform

children.description: players can use vehicles or mounts (e.g., horses, dragons, etc.) to traverse the world: these must  1. have their own controls and 2. be an alternative way of moving. Should apply to almost everything where the player controls a vehicle or mount.
children.name: mech: vehicles & mounts
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / mech: vehicles & mounts

children.description: mini-games that reward players with progress in some form
children.name: minigames
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / minigames

children.description:
minigames that consist of busywork (see "busywork" tag), e.g. trivial and repetitive tasks

minigames are generally expected to be different from the main game - for example in terms of input/movement
children.name: mini: busywork
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / minigames / mini: busywork

children.description:
includes minigames that can primarily be solved by employing logic, e.g Towers of Hanoi

minigames are generally expected to be different from the main game - for example in terms of input/movement
children.name: mini: logic
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / minigames / mini: logic

evidence.description: The game contains a minigame (in the trash bin).

children.description:
minigames that are luck based like flipping a coin

minigames are generally expected to be different from the main game - for example in terms of input/movement
children.name: mini: luck
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / minigames / mini: luck

children.description:
includes minigames that can primarily be solved through skill, e.g clicking objects in a short time frame in the correct order

minigames are generally expected to be different from the main game - for example in terms of input/movement
children.name: mini: skill
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / minigames / mini: skill

children.description:
minigames that require the player to do some strategic thinking

minigames are generally expected to be different from the main game - for example in terms of input/movement
children.name: mini: strategy
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / minigames / mini: strategy

children.description: multiplayer mechanics and categories
children.name: multiplayer
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer

children.description: players can leave behind artifacts in a shared game world
children.name: leave behind artifacts
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer / leave behind artifacts

children.description: players can leave behind objects in the shared (multiplayer) world
children.name: mul: leave game objects
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer / leave behind artifacts / mul: leave game objects

children.description: players can add (persistent) text to a shared (multiplayer) game world
children.name: mul: leave text
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer / leave behind artifacts / mul: leave text

children.description: (cooperative) multiplayer that is played in the same location, using a single device
children.name: mul: couch coop
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer / mul: couch coop

children.description: multiplayer that is played in the same network (LAN), using multiple devices
children.name: mul: local multiplayer
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer / mul: local multiplayer

children.description: multiplayer that is played online (using multiple devices)
children.name: mul: online multiplayer
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / multiplayer / mul: online multiplayer

children.description: mechanics and concepts to influence the replayability of a game
children.name: replayability
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability

children.description: how randomness is used inside the game (to enhance replayability)
children.name: randomness
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness

children.description: the outcome of an ability/skill is randomized to some degree
children.name: rnd: ability outcome
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: ability outcome

children.description: which abilities can be selected is randomized to some degree
children.name: rnd: ability select
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: ability select

children.description: how (AI) agents are spawned or how they behave is randomized to some degree
children.name: rnd: agents
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: agents

children.description: how enemies are spawned or how they behave is randomized to some degree
children.name: rnd: enemies
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: enemies

children.description: random events can occur during gameplay (e.g. natural disasters or NPC quests)
children.name: rnd: events
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: events

children.description: loot (items that enemies drop or that can be found in chests) is randomized to some degree, e.g., using a loot table
children.name: rnd: loot
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: loot

children.description: the game world is randomly generated to some degree, e.g., procedural generation
children.name: rnd: world generation
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / randomness / rnd: world generation

children.description:
the game provides mechanics that result in different ways of playing the game (e.g. skilltree)

this does not apply to scenarios where the core gameplay is the same but with a slightly different flavor (e.g. pokemon)
children.name: rep: gameplay branches
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / rep: gameplay branches

children.description: players progress after finishing a game/match (e.g. increasing a character level in multiplayer games, etc.)
children.name: rep: meta progression
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / rep: meta progression

children.description: players can make different choices for the narrative, resulting in different outcomes
children.name: rep: narrative branches
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / rep: narrative branches

children.description: game includes a scoring or ranking system that may motivate players to replay (sections of) the game
children.name: rep: scoring system
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / rep: scoring system

children.description: players unlock new content after finishing the game or a run of the game, e.g. new levels, new cosmetics, new game modes, story elements, etc.
children.name: rep: unlock content
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / replayability / rep: unlock content

children.description: mechanics related to building
children.name: schemata
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / schemata

children.description:
players can define or build blueprints that function as a planning help.
This can for example refer to a UI in which the player can plan what he wishes to do in the future.
children.name: sch: blueprint
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / schemata / sch: blueprint

children.description: players can use copy-paste features for building for example selecting an area that is copied to another location.
children.name: sch: copy-paste
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / schemata / sch: copy-paste

children.description:
the game includes recipes, e.g., for crafting
A recipe would contain a list of items that are required for crafting.
children.name: sch: recipe
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / schemata / sch: recipe

children.description: players can define (building) templates to reuse at another point in time. For example when creating a building and saving it to be build a second time later on.
children.name: sch: template
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / schemata / sch: template

children.description: different types of systems that games can simulate, which have an impact on other mechanics
children.name: simulation systems
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / simulation systems

children.description: agents in the game have simulated daily routines
children.name: sim: daily routines
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / simulation systems / sim: daily routines

children.description: the game includes a day/night cycle or meaningful connection of daytime to other game mechanics
children.name: sim: day-night cycle
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / simulation systems / sim: day-night cycle

children.description: the game features a simulated (even if simple) economy with supply and demand
children.name: sim: economy
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / simulation systems / sim: economy

children.description: the game simulates needs of people, animals, plants, etc.
children.name: sim: needs
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / simulation systems / sim: needs

children.description: the game simulates weather or seasons in some way meaningful to other game mechanics
children.name: sim: seasons & weather
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / simulation systems / sim: seasons & weather

children.description: the game models vision (player or AI agents see something) in some way
children.name: vision
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / vision

children.description: vision works through cones (visible)
children.name: vision: cones
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / vision / vision: cones

children.description:
game has fog of war, i.e., vision is limited to some area around players and other items/units/objects that give vision
this can include unexplored areas on a map being hidden
children.name: vision: fog of war
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / vision / vision: fog of war

children.description: game includes some other form of vision model
children.name: vision: other
children.pathNames: form / mechanics / vision / vision: other
